text: customer self repair hewlett packard enterprise products are designed with many customer self repair (csr) parts to minimize repair time and allow for greater flexibility in performing defective parts replacement. if during the diagnosis period hewlett packard enterprise (or hewlett packard enterprise service providers or service partners) identifies that the repair can be accomplished by the use of a csr part, hewlett packard enterprise will ship that part directly to you for replacement. there are two categories of csr parts: mandatory -parts for which customer self repair is mandatory. if you request hewlett packard enterprise to replace these parts, you will be charged for the travel and labor costs of this service. optional -parts for which customer self repair is optional. these parts are also designed for customer self repair. if, however, you require that hewlett packard enterprise replace them for you, there may or may not be additional charges, depending on the type of warranty service designated for your product. some hewlett packard enterprise parts are not designed for customer self repair. in order to satisfy the customer warranty,

section_path: removing and replacing the friction rack rails
text: removing and replacing the friction rack rails if you are replacing the rack mounting rails from a threaded-hole rack, make sure that you have a t-25 torx screwdriver available. power down the server. remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. disconnect all peripheral cables from the server. if installed, remove the cable management arm. remove the server from the rack. place the server on a flat, level work surface. to remove the sliding rails, do the following: slide the rail towards the front panel and pull it from the server. repeat steps a and b to remove the other sliding rail. to remove the rack mounting rails, do the following: in a threaded-hole rack, remove the rail screws. press and hold the release latch, and then disengage the rail pins from the rack columns. repeat steps a and b to remove the other rack rail. the removal procedure is complete. to replace the component, reverse this procedure.

text: removing and replacing an expansion card to reduce the risk of personal injury, electric shock, or damage to the equipment, remove power from the server by removing the power cord. the front panel power on/standby button does not shut off system power. portions of the power supply and some internal circuitry remain active until ac power is removed. power down the server. remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. extend the server from the rack. remove the server from the rack. remove the expansion card from the riser cage. to replace the component, reverse the removal procedure. to reduce the risk of personal injury from hot surfaces, allow the drives and the internal system components to cool before touching them. power down the server. remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. extend the server from the rack. remove the server from the rack. if needed, disconnect the gpu power cable and then remove the gpu from the riser.

section_path: removing and replacing a processor or heatsink
text: removing and replacing a processor or heatsink identify the heatsink and processor socket components. before you perform this procedure, make sure that you have the following items available: to reduce the risk of personal injury from hot surfaces, allow the drives, power supplies, and internal system components to cool before touching them. a discharge of static electricity from a finger or other conductor might damage system boards or other static-sensitive devices. to prevent damage, observe antistatic precautions. power down the server. remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. disconnect all peripheral cables from the server. if the server is installed in an enclosure or a rack, remove the server and place it on a flat, level work surface. remove the air baffle and all components that block access to the heatsink and processor. allow all internal system components to cool before continuing. loosen one pair of diagonally opposite heatsink screws, and then loosen the other pair of heatsink screws. heatsink screws must be tightened and loosened in alternating sequence.

section_path: re-entering the server serial number and product id
text: re-entering the server serial number and product id after replacing the system board, re-enter the system serial number and product id, and then configure the date and time settings. access the uefi system utilities. during post, press f9 . from the system utilities screen, select system configuration > bios/platform configuration (rbsu) > advanced options > advanced service options . select serial number , and then press enter . the serial number is modified by qualified service personnel and must match the serial number located on the chassis. product id is modified only by qualified service personnel. this value must match the product id located on the chassis. from the system utilities screen, select system configuration > bios/platform configuration (rbsu) > date and time . reconfigure the system date and time settings. to confirm and save the settings, press f12 .

source_file: HPE_sd00002445en_us_HPE ProLiant DL360 Gen11 Server Maintenance and Service Guide.pdf
section_path: reconfiguring the system date and time settings
text: reconfiguring the system date and time settings access the uefi system utilities. during post, press f9 . from the system utilities screen, select system configuration > bios/platform configuration (rbsu) > date and time . select a setting, and then complete your entry. date (mm-dd-yyyy) -enter the date in a month-day-year (mm-dd-yyyy) format. hour format -select either a 12- or 24-hours format. coordinated universal time (utc) -calculates the time stored in the hardware real-time clock (rtc) from the associated time zone setting. local time -removes the use of the time zone setting. this option is useful for addressing interaction issues in windows operating systems set in legacy bios boot mode. enabled -adjusts the local time displayed by one hour for daylight savings time. disabled -does not adjust the local time displayed for daylight savings time. to confirm and save the settings, press f12 . troubleshooting resources are available for hpe gen11 server products in the following documents: troubleshooting guide for hpe proliant gen11 servers provides procedures for resolving common problems and comprehensive courses of action for fault isolation and identification, issue

source_file: HPE_sd00002445en_us_HPE ProLiant DL360 Gen11 Server Maintenance and Service Guide.pdf
text: worn, wash hands with plenty of water after cleanup. spill or leak of the electrically conductive server coolant from the liquid cooling module. the supply hose of the liquid cooling module is damaged. have the following items ready for the coolant leak cleanup: dry paper towels or any absorbent material intended for cleaning up a chemical spill inspect the server room first and determine if the spill has spread to other servers in the same rack. power down the server. if the server uses a dlc cold plate module, disconnect the dlc hoses from the rack manifolds. if installed, open the cable management arm. to reduce the risk of electric shock, make sure that you use the necessary safety equipment compliant with local occupational health and safety code when disconnecting the power cords.

source_file: HPE_sd00002445en_us_HPE ProLiant DL360 Gen11 Server Maintenance and Service Guide.pdf
text: remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. disconnect all peripheral cables from the server. remove the server from the rack. place the server on a flat, level work surface. look for any potential contact between the coolant and any of the internal cables and components, especially power connectors. cover the spill point with a dry absorbent material. remove the leaky liquid cooling module. avoid pushing out more coolant during removal. remove the system board. if the leak has made it to the system board or the chassis, do the following: lightly dampen a cleanroom wipe with deionized water. wring out any excess water, and gently wipe over areas with coolant residue. ensure that there is no more visible colored coolant or liquid residue. dry the system board in a 70°c environment for at least 8 hours. confirm the system board is completely dry before reinstallation. follow the procedure in the server maintenance and service guide to reinstall the system board. repeat steps 12-19 on other servers affected by the
